caden_hsu.name: Caden Hsu
caden_hsu.summary:
Representing Washington, Caden Hsu is in the early chapters of a competitive journey, currently competing at the U19 level, with 3 entries across 1 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

A true triple-threat, Caden competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).
caden_hsu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Caden Hsu, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Caden has 3 career entries across 1 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Caden saga began at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 13th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Caden plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 3 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

At Junior Nationals, Caden has shown up 3 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Caden has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Caden showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U19, which means Caden traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Caden entered 3 events with results ranging from 13th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 21st, which is a polite way of saying Caden paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Caden is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Caden wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Caden, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

caden_wang.name: Caden Wang
caden_wang.summary:
Out of California, Caden Wang is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-3 finish at Selection places Caden in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

A true triple-threat, Caden competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-3 (U15 XD) • Junior Nationals top-4 (BD U15) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Caden has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Caden has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Caden is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
caden_wang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Caden Wang, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-3. Top-3 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 72 career entries in 25 tournaments, Caden has 1 title — a 1% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 71 entries where Caden drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 72 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Caden saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC in BS U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Caden's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 16 entries — a stretch where Caden actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Caden is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Caden would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Caden has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Caden has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Caden's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Caden plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Caden is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. And then there are the partners — 21 of them, to be precise. Caden has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Caden's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Jessica Wu: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Jessica Wu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U17 — we hope Jessica Wu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Caden's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Caden entered 12 events with results ranging from top-3 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Caden's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Caden and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Caden 12 times, with a best of top-4. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Caden is not just losing locally — Caden is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 1% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wang family travels cross-country so Caden can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Caden has 33 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Caden showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U15, which means Caden traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 NW ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Caden entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Caden paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 72 entries, and 71 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Caden looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Caden's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Caden has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Caden. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

caitlynn_ningxi_kurniawan.name: Caitlynn Ningxi Kurniawan
caitlynn_ningxi_kurniawan.summary:
Representing Texas, Caitlynn Ningxi Kurniawan has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U17 level, reaching the finals 1 time. With 14 entries across 5 tournaments, Caitlynn is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

Notably, Caitlynn has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Caitlynn especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

With 8 different doubles partners over the years, Caitlynn adapts to anyone:
  • Serena Yang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Caitlynn has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Caitlynn's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
caitlynn_ningxi_kurniawan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Caitlynn Ningxi Kurniawan, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Caitlynn has 14 career entries across 5 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Caitlynn saga began at 2022 South Frisco ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Caitlynn plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 14 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Caitlynn has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Caitlynn's texts. The go-to partner is Serena Yang (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 South Frisco ORC in XD U15 — we hope Vineet Mahadevan has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Caitlynn has shown up 2 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Caitlynn has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Caitlynn has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Caitlynn has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Caitlynn showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Caitlynn entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Caitlynn paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Caitlynn keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Caitlynn has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Caitlynn, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Caitlynn. We will be here, taking notes.

What makes Cali especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 9 different doubles partners over the years, Cali adapts to anyone:
  • Pratham Bansal (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Arushi Panda — 3 events in GD, best: #2, across 2 seasons

Cali has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Cali brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
cali_bennett.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Cali Bennett, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 27 events across 11 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 27-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Cali's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Cali keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Cali saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Cali experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Cali has reached the finals 2 times — at 2023 Egret Midwest OLC, 2024 Midwest CRC — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Cali has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Cali plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 27 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Cali has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Cali's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Pratham Bansal: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Pratham Bansal has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17 — we hope Jonas Prytz has emotionally recovered.

Cali has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Cali has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Cali showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17, which means Cali traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Cali entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Cali paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 27 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Cali. Cali just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Cali has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Cali. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Cali: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

callie_chen.roast:

This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 62 career entries in 22 tournaments, Callie has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 61 entries where Callie drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 62 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Callie saga began at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 62 entries, zero titles in any format. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Callie's texts. The go-to partner is Emily Zhang (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Justin Zhou has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Callie entered 3 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Callie's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Callie and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Callie has shown up 9 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Callie is not just losing locally — Callie is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chen family travels cross-country so Callie can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Callie entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Callie paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 62 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Callie. Callie just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Callie has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Callie. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Callie: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 24 entries — a stretch where Camellia actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Camellia is 0-for-4. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Camellia would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 9 times Camellia has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 9 times Camellia has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Camellia's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Camellia plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Camellia is 0-for-20. Zero titles in 20 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Camellia and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Camellia needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Camellia has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Camellia's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Alex Wen: 13 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 13 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Alex Wen has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U13 — we hope Alex Wen has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Camellia entered 5 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Camellia's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Camellia and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Camellia has shown up 9 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Camellia has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Camellia is not just losing locally — Camellia is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wang family travels cross-country so Camellia can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Camellia has 29 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Camellia showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U13, which means Camellia traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Camellia entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Camellia paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 69 entries, and 67 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Camellia looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Camellia's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Camellia has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Camellia. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

cameron_shanyu_jiang.name: Cameron Shanyu Jiang
cameron_shanyu_jiang.summary: Cameron Shanyu Jiang has stepped onto the competitive stage with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Cameron's.
cameron_shanyu_jiang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Cameron Shanyu Jiang, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Cameron is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 NorCal ORC, and so far, the only thing Cameron has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Cameron saga began at 2022 NorCal ORC with 1 entries and a best of 65th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Cameron is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Cameron's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Cameron is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Cameron has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Cameron showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Cameron traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Cameron is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Cameron wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Cameron, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

carina_huang.name: Carina Huang
carina_huang.summary:
Out of California, Carina Huang has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Carina's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Carina has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Carina competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (9 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Carina has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 18 different players across the career:
  • Lilian Shu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U19 (6 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Matthew Li (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U19/XD (3 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Thomas Wang (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #5)
  • Harshita Kandambakkam (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #5)

Carina has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Carina has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Carina brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
carina_huang.roast:

This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 48 career entries in 16 tournaments, Carina has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 47 entries where Carina drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 48 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Carina saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Carina experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Carina has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 SoCal Arena CRC, where Carina entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U17, XD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. And remember: Carina has ZERO titles. None. 4 times in the final, zero trophies to show for it. This is the most committed bridesmaid in the history of junior badminton.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Carina plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 48 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 18 of them, to be precise. Carina has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Carina's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Lilian Shu: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Lilian Shu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in GD U17 — we hope Hannah George has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Carina entered 9 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Carina's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Carina and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Carina has shown up 6 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Carina has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Carina is not just losing locally — Carina is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Huang family travels cross-country so Carina can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-17 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Carina has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Carina showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U19, which means Carina traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Carina entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 17th. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Carina paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 48 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Carina. Carina just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Carina has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Carina. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Carina: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 3 titles in 14 entries — a stretch where Carissa actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 2 entries. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Carissa's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U19 — we hope Srijan Challapalli has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Carissa's 4 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Carissa has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Carissa entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Carissa paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 22 entries, and 18 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Carissa looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Carissa's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Carissa has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Carissa. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

carsten_lam.name: Carsten Lam
carsten_lam.summary:
Representing Texas, Carsten Lam has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-3 in BD U15, BD U13. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Carsten belongs with the best in the country.

Carsten is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Carsten especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (12 entries) • Junior Nationals top-3 (BD U15, BD U13) • 3 ORC titles.

With 22 different doubles partners over the years, Carsten adapts to anyone:
  • Sophia Sun (current partner) — currently teaming up in U13/XD (11 events, best: #2, spanning 5 seasons)
  • Kaitlyn Kim (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
  • Abhiram Tripuraneni (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5)
  • Anthony Cheng Xu (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in BD

Carsten is peaking at the right time in 2025-2026, with 1 title across 3 tournaments — a clear sign that Carsten is still ascending.

Carsten has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Carsten has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Carsten has been seeded #1 or #2 in 6 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 3 titles, Carsten is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
carsten_lam.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Carsten Lam, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 90 career entries in 31 tournaments, Carsten has 3 titles — a 3% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 29 entries where Carsten drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 90 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Carsten saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2023 Midwest ORC in BD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Carsten's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Carsten experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Carsten has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 Dave Freeman Jr OLC, where Carsten entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (BS U13, XD U13, BD U13). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Carsten does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Carsten has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 3 wins means Carsten is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Carsten plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Carsten is 0-for-27. Zero titles in 27 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Carsten and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Carsten needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Carsten has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Carsten's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Sophia Sun: 11 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 11 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Sophia Sun has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Cayden Wang has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Carsten entered 12 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Carsten's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Carsten and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Carsten 9 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Then there is the seeding situation. Carsten has been seeded in 13 events and underperformed the seeding 5 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Carsten takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U11, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Carsten. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Carsten of is playing it safe. Carsten has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Carsten has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 3 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Carsten has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Carsten is not just losing locally — Carsten is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lam family travels cross-country so Carsten can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Carsten has 30 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Carsten showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U15, which means Carsten traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Carsten entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Carsten paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 90 entries, and 87 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Carsten looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Carsten's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Carsten has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Carsten. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

cassidy_yeh.name: Cassidy Yeh
cassidy_yeh.summary:
Representing California, Cassidy Yeh has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U15 XD, U15 GD, U19 GD, U17 XD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 12 career titles across 26 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Cassidy is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (9 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Cassidy competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 5 doubles titles, 4 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U15 XD, U15 GD, U19 GD, U17 XD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (XD U19) • 5 ORC titles.

With 13 different doubles partners over the years, Cassidy adapts to anyone:
  • Zoey Enqi Tan (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (16 total events in GD/U15/U19, spanning 5 seasons)
  • Justin Xia (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (5 total events in U17/XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Zicheng Xu — 1 events, 1 title together in XD

Cassidy has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Cassidy has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 8 seeded entries across the career, Cassidy consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 6 seasons and 12 titles, Cassidy has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
cassidy_yeh.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Cassidy Yeh, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Cassidy is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Cassidy has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Cassidy would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Cassidy, we kept reading — all 71 entries across 26 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 17% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Cassidy held up a trophy, there were roughly 4 other times Cassidy walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Cassidy saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2022 Dave Freeman OLC in XD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Cassidy's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 3 titles in 17 entries — a stretch where Cassidy actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 4 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Cassidy experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Cassidy has reached 9 championship matches across this career. That is 9 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 9 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (8 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Cassidy does own 12 actual titles, so it is not like Cassidy has never won anything. But 9 finals losses versus 12 wins means Cassidy is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Cassidy plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 5 doubles, 4 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Cassidy can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Cassidy has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Cassidy's texts. The most successful partnership is with Zoey Enqi Tan — 16 events, 6 titles — and honestly, Zoey Enqi Tan deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Zoey Enqi Tan a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U17 — we hope Max Zhang has emotionally recovered.

One thing nobody can accuse Cassidy of is playing it safe. Cassidy has entered older age groups at 9 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Cassidy has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 12 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Cassidy has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Cassidy is not just losing locally — Cassidy is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 17% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yeh family travels cross-country so Cassidy can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Cassidy has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Cassidy showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in GS U15, which means Cassidy traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Cassidy entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Cassidy paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 12 titles to the name, 71 entries, and 59 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Cassidy looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Cassidy's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Cassidy has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Cassidy. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

catherine_chen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Catherine Chen, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Catherine saga began at 2024 Midwest CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 2nd. But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Catherine experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Catherine has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 33 entries, and 29 losses. catherine_qin.name: Catherine Qin
catherine_qin.summary:
Hailing from Illinois, Catherine Qin has claimed a championship, currently competing at the U19 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 10 entries across 5 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Catherine is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Catherine is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Catherine has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Kristin Yingxin Chen (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in GD

The 2023-2024 season tells a story of acceleration, with 1 title across 2 tournaments — a clear sign that Catherine is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Catherine has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Catherine's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
catherine_qin.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Catherine Qin, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 1 win across 10 entries in 5 tournaments. That is a 10% conversion rate, which means roughly 9 out of every 10 times Catherine shows up to a tournament, Catherine pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Catherine saga began at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2024 Midwest CRC in GD U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Catherine's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Catherine plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Catherine is 0-for-5. Zero titles in 5 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Catherine and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Catherine needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Catherine's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

One thing nobody can accuse Catherine of is playing it safe. Catherine has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U15, U17, U19 — which means Catherine has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 1 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Catherine has built a permanent residence on that line.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Catherine has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Catherine showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U17, which means Catherine traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Catherine entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 21st, which is a polite way of saying Catherine paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 10 entries, and 9 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Catherine looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Catherine's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Catherine has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Catherine. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Notably, Cayden has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Cayden is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 3 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U13 XD) • Junior Nationals top-3 (XD U13, BD U13, BS U13) • 5 ORC titles.

With 23 different doubles partners over the years, Cayden adapts to anyone:
  • Celine Ao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Ayaaz Muhammed Baig (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #4)

Cayden has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Cayden has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 24 seeded entries across the career, Cayden consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 6 seasons and 7 titles, Cayden has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
cayden_wang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Cayden Wang, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-4. Top-4 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 89 career entries in 30 tournaments, Cayden has 7 titles — a 8% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 11 entries where Cayden drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 89 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Cayden saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 7th. The first title would not come until 2021 NorCal CRC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Cayden's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 4 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Cayden actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Cayden is 0-for-12. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Cayden plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 2 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Cayden can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 23 of them, to be precise. Cayden has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Cayden's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Rishabh Praveen: 9 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 9 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Rishabh Praveen has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Carsten Lam has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Cayden entered 9 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Cayden's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Cayden and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Cayden 11 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Cayden has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Cayden is not just losing locally — Cayden is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 8% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wang family travels cross-country so Cayden can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Cayden has 22 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Cayden showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U15, which means Cayden traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Cayden entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Cayden paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 7 titles to the name, 89 entries, and 82 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Cayden looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Cayden's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Cayden has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Cayden. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.
