celina_tai.name: Celina Tai
celina_tai.summary:
Hailing from Illinois, Celina Tai is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U15 level, with 10 entries across 5 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

What makes Celina especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Celina has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Celina brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
celina_tai.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Celina Tai, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 10 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 10-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Celina's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Celina keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Celina saga began at 2023 Midwest ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Celina plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 10 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Celina has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Celina showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U13, which means Celina traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 MBC Midwest OLC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Celina entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Celina paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Celina keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Celina has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Celina, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Celina. We will be here, taking notes.

celine_ao.name: Celine Ao
celine_ao.summary:
Out of Washington, Celine Ao has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-3 in GD U11 - Position 1-4. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Celine belongs with the best in the country.

Celine is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Celine competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (12 entries) • Junior Nationals top-3 (GD U11 - Position 1-4) • ORC top-2 finisher.

With 30 different doubles partners over the years, Celine adapts to anyone:
  • Cayden Wang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Emily Min Tang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U15 (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Felicia Ho (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Having moved up to U17, Celine is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Celine brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Celine has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Celine has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience and 2 titles, Celine is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
celine_ao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Celine Ao, from Washington, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 104 career entries in 36 tournaments, Celine has 2 titles — a 2% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 51 entries where Celine drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 104 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Celine saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2024 OBA NW OLC in GD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Celine's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 18 entries — a stretch where Celine actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Celine is 0-for-12. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Celine experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Celine has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Celine does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Celine has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 2 wins means Celine is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Celine plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Celine can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 30 of them, to be precise. Celine has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Celine's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Nathaniel Volfbeyn: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Nathaniel Volfbeyn has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U13 — we hope Jason Yang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Celine's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Celine entered 12 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Celine's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Celine and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Celine 12 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

One thing nobody can accuse Celine of is playing it safe. Celine has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Celine has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 2 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Celine has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Celine is not just losing locally — Celine is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ao family travels cross-country so Celine can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-49 finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Celine has 44 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Celine showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U15, which means Celine traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Celine entered 3 events with results ranging from 7th to 49th. That is an average placement of 24th, which is a polite way of saying Celine paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 104 entries, and 102 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Celine looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Celine's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Celine has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Celine. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Chang plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Chang is 0-for-14. Zero titles in 14 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Chang and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Chang needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Chang has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Chang's texts. The go-to partner is Jessica Wu (16 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U15 — we hope Layton Wen has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Chang entered 5 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Chang's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Chang and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Chang 9 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

One thing nobody can accuse Chang of is playing it safe. Chang has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U15, U17, U19 — which means Chang has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 1 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Chang has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Chang is not just losing locally — Chang is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The He family travels cross-country so Chang can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Chang has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Chang showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in GS U15, which means Chang traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Chang entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 23rd, which is a polite way of saying Chang paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 51 entries, and 50 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Chang looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Chang's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Chang has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Chang. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

charity_lam.name: Charity Lam
charity_lam.summary:
Hailing from Texas, Charity Lam wears the gold from Junior Nationals — the single most important tournament for any American junior player in GS U19, GD U19. That title represents the culmination of grueling competition against the very best in the country. With 10 career victories across 31 tournaments, Charity has proven the ability to deliver when it matters most.

Notably, Charity has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Charity competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 3 singles titles, 4 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (12 entries) • Junior Nationals champion (GS U19, GD U19) • 5 ORC titles.

With 19 different doubles partners over the years, Charity adapts to anyone:
  • Lydia Chao (current primary partner) — 7 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (10 total events in GD/U19, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Ansen Liu (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (6 total events in XD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Suraj Rajesh Kumar — a trusted U19/XD partner (8 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 6 podium finishes across 3 tournaments. Charity is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Charity has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Charity has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 7 seeded entries across the career, Charity consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 10 titles, Charity is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
charity_lam.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Charity Lam, from Texas, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. And yes, Charity IS a Junior National Champion — GS U19, GD U19 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 89-entry career across 31 tournaments with a 11% win rate, which means 79 times — 79! — Charity went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 89 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Charity saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2022 NorCal ORC in GD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Charity's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 6 titles in 23 entries — a stretch where Charity actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 9 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Charity experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Charity has reached 8 championship matches across this career. That is 8 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 8 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Charity entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U19, GD U19). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (5 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Charity does own 10 actual titles, so it is not like Charity has never won anything. But 8 finals losses versus 10 wins means Charity is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Charity plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 4 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Charity can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 19 of them, to be precise. Charity has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Charity's texts. The go-to partner is Lydia Chao (10 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U15 — we hope Justin Kan has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Charity entered 12 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Charity's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Charity and said 'nah.' Tournament committees have handed Charity a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Charity has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in GS U19, where Charity was seeded #2 and finished 17th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Charity has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Charity is not just losing locally — Charity is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 11% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lam family travels cross-country so Charity can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Charity has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Charity showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in GS U15, which means Charity traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Charity entered 3 events with results ranging from 1st to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Charity paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 10 titles to the name, 89 entries, and 79 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Charity looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Charity's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Charity has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Charity. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

charles_lin.name: Charles Lin
charles_lin.summary:
Out of Texas, Charles Lin is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U11 level, with 3 entries across 1 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

What makes Charles especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
charles_lin.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Charles Lin, from Texas, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 3 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Charles is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC (2025-26), and so far, the only thing Charles has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Charles saga began at 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC (2025-26) with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Charles plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 3 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Charles has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Charles showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC (2025-26) in BS U11, which means Charles traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC (2025-26) in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC (2025-26), where Charles entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Charles paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Charles is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Charles wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Charles, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

charlie_chen.summary:
Hailing from California, Charlie Chen is a consistent semifinalist, currently competing at the U17 level, reaching the final four 3 times. That kind of reliability at the top of the draw is the signature of a player who will soon be contending for titles.

A true triple-threat, Charlie competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (1 entry) • Junior Nationals competitor (7 entries).

With 26 different doubles partners over the years, Charlie adapts to anyone:
  • Niya Olivia Chen (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Charlie has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Charlie has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Charlie brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
charlie_chen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Charlie Chen, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-33. Top-33 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 49 career entries in 21 tournaments, Charlie has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 48 entries where Charlie drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 49 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Charlie saga began at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Charlie plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 49 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 26 of them, to be precise. Charlie has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Charlie's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U15 — we hope Richard Liu has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Charlie has shown up 7 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Charlie is not just losing locally — Charlie is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chen family travels cross-country so Charlie can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Charlie has 37 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Charlie showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15, which means Charlie traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Charlie entered 2 events with results ranging from 49th to 65th. That is an average placement of 57th, which is a polite way of saying Charlie paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 49 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Charlie. Charlie just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Charlie has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Charlie. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Charlie: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Charlie paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 40 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Charlie. Charlie just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Charlie has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Charlie. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Charlie: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Charlie is right on schedule. The Charlie saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Charlie is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Charlie's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Charlie is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Charlie has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Charlie showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2021 NorCal CRC in BS U11, which means Charlie traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Charlie is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Charlie wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Charlie, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

charlotte_a_tse.name: Charlotte A Tse
charlotte_a_tse.summary:
Representing California, Charlotte A Tse has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Charlotte's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

A true triple-threat, Charlotte competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

Charlotte has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 6 different players across the career:
  • Ethan Paw (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U17/XD (5 events)
  • Tanvi Deshmukh (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #4)
  • Harshita Kandambakkam (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U17 (2 events, best: #5)

Charlotte has competed across 4 different regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Charlotte's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
charlotte_a_tse.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Charlotte A Tse, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 18 career entries in 6 tournaments, Charlotte has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 17 entries where Charlotte drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 18 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Charlotte saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 6th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Charlotte plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 18 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Charlotte has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Ethan Paw: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Ethan Paw has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17 — we hope Ethan Paw has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Charlotte entered 3 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Charlotte's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Charlotte and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Charlotte has shown up 3 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Charlotte is not just losing locally — Charlotte is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Tse family travels cross-country so Charlotte can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Charlotte has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Charlotte showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in GS U17, which means Charlotte traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Charlotte entered 3 events with results ranging from 4th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 23rd, which is a polite way of saying Charlotte paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Charlotte keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Charlotte has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Charlotte, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Charlotte. We will be here, taking notes.

charlotte_richards.name: Charlotte Richards
charlotte_richards.summary:
Out of Illinois, Charlotte Richards is a serial champion on the local circuit, currently competing at the U19 level, racking up 6 titles across 18 tournaments. That winning pedigree at the local and club level creates the perfect launchpad for success at the regional and national stages.

Notably, Charlotte has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Charlotte competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 4 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (6 entries).

Charlotte has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 16 different players across the career:
  • Kaitlyn Kim (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in GD
  • Sophia Lin — 7 events in GD, producing 3 titles together, spanning 3 seasons

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 2 tournaments. Charlotte is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Charlotte has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Charlotte isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Over 5 seasons and 6 titles, Charlotte has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
charlotte_richards.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Charlotte Richards, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 53 career entries in 18 tournaments, Charlotte has 6 titles — a 11% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 7 entries where Charlotte drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 53 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Charlotte saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2023 Egret Midwest OLC in XD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Charlotte's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Charlotte experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Charlotte has reached 7 championship matches across this career. That is 7 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 7 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 Egret Midwest OLC, where Charlotte entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U15, GD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (4 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Charlotte does own 6 actual titles, so it is not like Charlotte has never won anything. But 7 finals losses versus 6 wins means Charlotte is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Charlotte plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 4 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Charlotte can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Charlotte has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Charlotte's texts. The go-to partner is Aaron Fu (9 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Aaron Fu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Charlotte's 6 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Charlotte entered 6 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Charlotte's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Charlotte and said 'nah.'

Charlotte has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Charlotte has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Charlotte has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Charlotte showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U17, which means Charlotte traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Charlotte entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Charlotte paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 53 entries, and 47 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Charlotte looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Charlotte's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Charlotte has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Charlotte. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Charvi has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Charvi has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Charvi brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
charvi_murali.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Charvi Murali, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Charvi has 42 career entries across 17 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Charvi saga began at 2022 South Frisco ORC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Charvi experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Charvi has reached the finals 2 times — at 2025 Austin Leander South CRC, 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Charvi has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Charvi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 42 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Charvi has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Charvi's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Niranjan Manjith: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Niranjan Manjith has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Sashanth Senthil has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Charvi has shown up 5 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this. Then there is the seeding situation. Charvi has been seeded in 3 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Charvi takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC in GS U17, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Charvi. The evidence suggests they should not have.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Charvi is not just losing locally — Charvi is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Murali family travels cross-country so Charvi can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Charvi has 28 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Charvi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Charvi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Charvi entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Charvi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 42 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Charvi. Charvi just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Charvi has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Charvi. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Charvi: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

charvi_srivastava.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Charvi Srivastava, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-6. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Charvi has 71 career entries across 25 tournaments and has managed to win 9 of them — a 13% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Charvi saga began at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in GD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Charvi's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 6 titles in 20 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Charvi experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Charvi has reached 9 championship matches across this career. That is 9 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 9 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 Egret Midwest OLC, where Charvi entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U13, GD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (6 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Charvi does own 9 actual titles, so it is not like Charvi has never won anything. But 9 finals losses versus 9 wins means Charvi is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Charvi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 5 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Charvi can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 19 of them, to be precise. Charvi has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Charvi's texts. The most successful partnership is with Elena Zhang — 11 events, 4 titles — and honestly, Elena Zhang deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Elena Zhang a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U13 — we hope Matthew Wu has emotionally recovered.

Of Charvi's 9 career titles, 7 came at local events versus 2 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Charvi feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At Junior Nationals, Charvi has shown up 9 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6. Then there is the seeding situation. Charvi has been seeded in 9 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Charvi takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2023 Egret Midwest OLC in GS U13, and finishing 4th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Charvi. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Charvi has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Charvi is not just losing locally — Charvi is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 13% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Srivastava family travels cross-country so Charvi can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Charvi has 27 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Charvi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15, which means Charvi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 South ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Charvi entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Charvi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 9 titles to the name, 71 entries, and 62 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Charvi looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Charvi's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Charvi has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Charvi. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

chase_khuu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Chase Khuu, from California, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Chase is right on schedule. It all started at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, and so far, the only thing Chase has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Chase saga began at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC with 1 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Chase is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Chase's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Chase is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Chase has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Chase showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U11, which means Chase traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Chase is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Chase wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Chase, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

chelsie_cheng.name: Chelsie Cheng
chelsie_cheng.summary:
Out of Illinois, Chelsie Cheng is a multi-time champion on the junior circuit, currently competing at the U13 level, capturing 4 titles across 15 tournaments and 42 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Chelsie keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Chelsie is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Chelsie especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries).

Chelsie has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 16 different players across the career:
  • Catherine Du (current primary partner) — 7 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (7 total events in GD, across 2 seasons)
  • Yashas Cheerlavancha (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

Chelsie has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Chelsie isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Chelsie brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
chelsie_cheng.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Chelsie Cheng, from Illinois, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Chelsie has 42 career entries across 15 tournaments and has managed to win 4 of them — a 10% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Chelsie saga began at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2023 Egret Midwest OLC in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Chelsie's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Chelsie plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Chelsie can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Chelsie has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Chelsie's texts. The go-to partner is Catherine Du (7 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Vivaan Khullar has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Chelsie's 4 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Chelsie has shown up 9 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9. Then there is the seeding situation. Chelsie has been seeded in 4 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Chelsie takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC in GS U11, and finishing 5th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Chelsie. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Chelsie has also played up into older age groups at 3 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Chelsie has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Chelsie has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Chelsie showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13, which means Chelsie traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Chelsie entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Chelsie paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 42 entries, and 38 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Chelsie looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Chelsie's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Chelsie has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Chelsie. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.
