cheree_leung.name: Cheree Leung
cheree_leung.summary:
Hailing from California, Cheree Leung broke through with a tournament title, currently competing at the U15 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 48 entries across 19 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Notably, Cheree has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Cheree competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Cheree has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 9 different players across the career:
  • Ethan Yip Ly (current primary partner) — 11 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (11 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Cindy Li (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (10 events, best: #2, spanning 4 seasons)

Having moved up to U15, Cheree is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Cheree brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

Cheree has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Cheree has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 4 seasons and 1 title, Cheree has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
cheree_leung.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Cheree Leung, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Cheree has 48 career entries across 19 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 2% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Cheree saga began at 2022 Dave Freeman OLC with 1 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) in XD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Cheree's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 14 entries — a stretch where Cheree actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Cheree is 0-for-16. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Cheree experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Cheree has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 SoCal CRC, where Cheree entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U11, GD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. Now yes, Cheree does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Cheree has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 1 wins means Cheree is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Cheree plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Cheree is 0-for-17. Zero titles in 17 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Cheree and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Cheree needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Cheree has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Cheree's texts. The go-to partner is Ethan Yip Ly (11 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Ethan Yip Ly has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Cheree's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Cheree has shown up 2 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Cheree has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Cheree is not just losing locally — Cheree is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Leung family travels cross-country so Cheree can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Cheree has 23 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Cheree showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15, which means Cheree traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Cheree entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Cheree paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 48 entries, and 47 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Cheree looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Cheree's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Cheree has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Cheree. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

ching_chun_fan.name: Ching Chun Fan
ching_chun_fan.summary:
Out of California, Ching Chun Fan has claimed a championship, currently competing at the U13 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 18 entries across 6 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Ching is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Ching has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 8 different players across the career:
  • Cheree Leung (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Ethan Yip Ly (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (3 total events in BD, across 2 seasons)

Ching is peaking at the right time in 2022-2023, with 1 title across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Ching is still ascending.

Ching has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning NorCal, SoCal, South, Ching isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Ching's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
ching_chun_fan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ching Chun Fan, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 1 win across 18 entries in 6 tournaments. That is a 6% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Ching shows up to a tournament, Ching pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Ching saga began at 2022 SoCal Arena CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2022 SoCal CRC in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Ching's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Ching experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Ching has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 SoCal CRC, where Ching entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U11, XD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Ching does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Ching has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 1 wins means Ching is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ching plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Ching is 0-for-6. Zero titles in 6 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Ching and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Ching needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Ching has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Ching's texts. The go-to partner is Cheree Leung (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U13 — we hope Landon Ling has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Ching's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Ching has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ching has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ching showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U13, which means Ching traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Ching entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Ching paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 18 entries, and 17 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ching looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ching's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ching has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ching. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

chingpei_lin.name: Chingpei Lin
chingpei_lin.summary:
Hailing from Texas, Chingpei Lin has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U15 level, reaching the finals 2 times. With 65 entries across 23 tournaments, Chingpei is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

A true triple-threat, Chingpei competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

With 21 different doubles partners over the years, Chingpei adapts to anyone:
  • Wayne Chien (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Sitar Sanghani (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Haziqa H Mathani (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Chingpei has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Chingpei has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Chingpei brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
chingpei_lin.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Chingpei Lin, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Chingpei has 65 career entries across 23 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Chingpei saga began at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Chingpei experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Chingpei has reached the finals 2 times — at 2025 Austin Leander South CRC, 2024 DFW South OLC — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Chingpei has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Chingpei plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 65 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 21 of them, to be precise. Chingpei has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Chingpei's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Yeewei Yap: 11 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 11 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Yeewei Yap has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U13 — we hope Wayne Chien has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Chingpei has shown up 6 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Chingpei is not just losing locally — Chingpei is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lin family travels cross-country so Chingpei can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Chingpei has 30 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Chingpei showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U13, which means Chingpei traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Chingpei entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Chingpei paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 65 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Chingpei. Chingpei just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Chingpei has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Chingpei. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Chingpei: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

chiranjeev_deepak.name: Chiranjeev Deepak
chiranjeev_deepak.summary:
Representing California, Chiranjeev Deepak is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-4 finish at Selection places Chiranjeev in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

A true triple-threat, Chiranjeev competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U13 BD) • Junior Nationals top-4 (BD U13) • 2 ORC titles.

With 9 different doubles partners over the years, Chiranjeev adapts to anyone:
  • Anshu Gowda (current primary partner) — 10 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (15 total events in BD/U13, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Siddhi Thorat (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U13/XD (8 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 2 titles across 3 tournaments — a clear sign that Chiranjeev is still ascending.

Chiranjeev has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Chiranjeev has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 11 seeded entries across the career, Chiranjeev consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 3 titles, Chiranjeev is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
chiranjeev_deepak.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Chiranjeev Deepak, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-4. Top-4 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 46 career entries in 16 tournaments, Chiranjeev has 3 titles — a 7% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 14 entries where Chiranjeev drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 46 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Chiranjeev saga began at 2022 NW ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC in BD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Chiranjeev's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 2 titles in 9 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Chiranjeev plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Chiranjeev can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Chiranjeev has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Chiranjeev's texts. The go-to partner is Anshu Gowda (15 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U13 — we hope Anshu Gowda has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Chiranjeev entered 3 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-5. The Selection committee looked at Chiranjeev's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Chiranjeev and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Chiranjeev 9 times, with a best of top-4. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Chiranjeev is not just losing locally — Chiranjeev is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Deepak family travels cross-country so Chiranjeev can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Chiranjeev has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Chiranjeev showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U11, which means Chiranjeev traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Chiranjeev entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Chiranjeev paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 46 entries, and 43 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Chiranjeev looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Chiranjeev's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Chiranjeev has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Chiranjeev. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

chloe_a_kai.name: Chloe A Kai
chloe_a_kai.summary:
Hailing from California, Chloe A Kai is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-2 finish at Selection places Chloe in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Chloe is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (6 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Chloe especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 6 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U17 XD) • Junior Nationals top-2 (XD U15, XD U17) • 4 ORC titles.

Chloe has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 21 different players across the career:
  • Jaden Ke Hong Chen (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U17/XD (7 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Annie Meng (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (3 total events in GD/U17)
  • Yalan Zhu (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Sydney Li — a trusted GD partner (5 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

The 2024-2025 season has been a step up, with 2 titles across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Chloe is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Chloe has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Chloe has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 12 seeded entries across the career, Chloe consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 9 titles, Chloe has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
chloe_a_kai.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Chloe A Kai, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-2. Top-2 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 72 career entries in 26 tournaments, Chloe has 9 titles — a 12% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 7 entries where Chloe drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 72 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Chloe saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2021 Egret Midwest OLC in GS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Chloe's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 5 titles in 23 entries — a stretch where Chloe actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 11 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Chloe experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Chloe has reached 12 championship matches across this career. That is 12 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 12 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Chloe entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U17, GD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (7 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Chloe does own 9 actual titles, so it is not like Chloe has never won anything. But 12 finals losses versus 9 wins means Chloe is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Chloe plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 6 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Chloe can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 21 of them, to be precise. Chloe has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Chloe's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Jaden Ke Hong Chen: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Jaden Ke Hong Chen has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U15 — we hope Ish Modi has emotionally recovered.

Of Chloe's 9 career titles, 5 came at local events versus 4 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Chloe feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Chloe entered 6 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Chloe's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Chloe and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Chloe has competed 9 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one. Then there is the seeding situation. Chloe has been seeded in 12 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Chloe takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2022 SoCal Arena CRC in GS U15, and finishing 5th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Chloe. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Chloe of is playing it safe. Chloe has entered older age groups at 6 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Chloe has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 5 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Chloe has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Chloe is not just losing locally — Chloe is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 12% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Kai family travels cross-country so Chloe can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Chloe has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Chloe showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U15, which means Chloe traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 U.S. Selection Event, where Chloe entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Chloe paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 9 titles to the name, 72 entries, and 63 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Chloe looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Chloe's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Chloe has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Chloe. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

chloe_ho.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Chloe Ho, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Chloe is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Chloe has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Chloe would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Chloe, we kept reading — all 37 entries across 14 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 27% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Chloe held up a trophy, there were roughly 2 other times Chloe walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Chloe saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Chloe burst onto the scene with a title in GD U17 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Chloe has delivered on some of those promises: 10 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Chloe promised and what Chloe has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 5 titles in 15 entries — a stretch where Chloe actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Chloe is 0-for-5. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Chloe experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Chloe has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Chloe does own 10 actual titles, so it is not like Chloe has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 10 wins means Chloe is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Chloe plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Chloe is 0-for-9. Zero titles in 9 tries. The best singles result is a 4th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Chloe and suddenly the wins start appearing — 9 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Chloe needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Chloe has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Chloe's texts. The most successful partnership is with Ella Yishan Lin — 7 events, 4 titles — and honestly, Ella Yishan Lin deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Ella Yishan Lin a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting.

Chloe has also played up into older age groups at 5 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Chloe has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Chloe has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Chloe showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U19, which means Chloe traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U19. A pattern, not a blip.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 10 titles to the name, 37 entries, and 27 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Chloe looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Chloe's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Chloe has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Chloe. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Chloe has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Chloe has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Chloe's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
chloe_lim.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Chloe Lim, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Chloe has 31 career entries across 11 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Chloe saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Chloe plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 31 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Chloe has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Xiaoyue Liu: 10 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 10 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Xiaoyue Liu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U15 — we hope Benson Ngai has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Chloe has shown up 3 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Chloe is not just losing locally — Chloe is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lim family travels cross-country so Chloe can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Chloe has 22 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Chloe showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in GS U15, which means Chloe traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 South Frisco ORC, where Chloe entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Chloe paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Chloe keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Chloe has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Chloe, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Chloe. We will be here, taking notes.

chris_lin.name: Chris Lin
chris_lin.summary:
Out of Texas, Chris Lin has conquered the Open Regional Championship stage, currently competing at the U13 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

A true triple-threat, Chris competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 2 singles titles, 4 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (5 entries) • 1 ORC title.

Chris has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 6 different players across the career:
  • Eesha Vikram (current primary partner) — 11 events together this season alone with 3 titles, building serious momentum (12 total events in XD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Ayaan Kale (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (5 total events in BD)
  • Kaishi Yang (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Fonghong Lin (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in BD

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 6 titles across 7 tournaments — a clear sign that Chris is still ascending.

Chris has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning NE, NW, South, Chris isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Chris brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
chris_lin.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Chris Lin, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Chris has 40 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 9 of them — a 22% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Chris saga began at 2024 DFW South OLC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2025 Austin Leander South CRC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Chris's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 6 titles in 21 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Chris experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Chris has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC, where Chris entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U11, BD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. Now yes, Chris does own 9 actual titles, so it is not like Chris has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 9 wins means Chris is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Chris plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 4 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Chris can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Chris has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The most successful partnership is with Eesha Vikram — 12 events, 3 titles — and honestly, Eesha Vikram deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Eesha Vikram a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BD U13 — we hope Ayaan Kale has emotionally recovered.

Of Chris's 9 career titles, 8 came at local events versus 1 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Chris feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At Junior Nationals, Chris has shown up 5 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Chris has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Chris has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Chris showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U13, which means Chris traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Chris entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Chris paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 9 titles to the name, 40 entries, and 31 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Chris looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Chris's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Chris has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Chris. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wu family travels cross-country so Chris can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Chris has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Chris showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in BS U17, which means Chris traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Chris entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Chris paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 50 entries, and 49 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Chris looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Chris's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Chris has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Chris. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

christopher_lu.name: Christopher Lu
christopher_lu.summary:
Representing Oregon, Christopher Lu has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U19 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 22 entries in 11 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Christopher is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Christopher especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Christopher has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 6 different players across the career:
  • Kohta Ito — a trusted BD partner (4 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Christopher has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Christopher has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Christopher brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
christopher_lu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Christopher Lu, from Oregon, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Christopher has 22 career entries across 11 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Christopher saga began at 2022 NW Oregon OLC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Christopher plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 22 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Christopher has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Kohta Ito (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Angela Zhang has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Christopher has shown up 2 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

One thing nobody can accuse Christopher of is playing it safe. Christopher has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U15, U17, U19 — which means Christopher has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' The results in those older brackets have been... character-building. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Christopher has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Christopher is not just losing locally — Christopher is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lu family travels cross-country so Christopher can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Christopher has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Christopher showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NW ORC in BS U15, which means Christopher traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Christopher entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Christopher paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 22 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Christopher. Christopher just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Christopher has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Christopher. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Christopher: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

chuqi_julie_jin.name: Chuqi Julie Jin
chuqi_julie_jin.summary:
A California competitor, Chuqi Julie Jin is a committed competitor who keeps showing up, currently competing at the U15 level, with 33 career entries across 11 tournaments. In a sport that rewards persistence, Chuqi's commitment is the ultimate investment in future success.

A true triple-threat, Chuqi competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries).

With 13 different doubles partners over the years, Chuqi adapts to anyone:
  • Alan Ma (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, spanning 3 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Chuqi has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Chuqi has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Chuqi brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
chuqi_julie_jin.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Chuqi Julie Jin, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Chuqi has 33 career entries across 11 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Chuqi saga began at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 13th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Chuqi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 33 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Chuqi has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Chuqi's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Alan Ma: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Alan Ma has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Alan Ma has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Chuqi has shown up 9 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Chuqi is not just losing locally — Chuqi is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Jin family travels cross-country so Chuqi can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Chuqi has 26 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Chuqi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13, which means Chuqi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Chuqi entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Chuqi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 33 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Chuqi. Chuqi just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Chuqi has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Chuqi. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Chuqi: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.
