cindy_zhou.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Cindy Zhou, from Washington, currently competing at the U11 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-3. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Cindy has 7 career entries across 3 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Cindy saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Cindy would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Cindy has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Cindy has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Cindy's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Cindy plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 7 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Junior Nationals has seen Cindy 3 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Cindy has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Cindy has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

Season after season, Cindy keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Cindy has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Cindy, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Cindy. We will be here, taking notes.

cinra_mei.name: Cinra Mei
cinra_mei.summary:
Hailing from California, Cinra Mei has conquered the Open Regional Championship stage, currently competing at the U17 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

What makes Cinra especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: 1 ORC title.

Cinra is peaking at the right time in 2024-2025, with 1 title across 3 tournaments — a clear sign that Cinra is still ascending.

With tournaments spanning NW, NorCal, SoCal, Cinra isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Cinra's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
cinra_mei.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Cinra Mei, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. An Open Regional Championship titlist with 1 ORC title, which sounds very official — and it is, ORCs are the real deal. But let's zoom out: that's 1 wins out of 10 ORC entries, and an overall career of 1-for-10 (10%). Regional royalty, national afterthought. A big deal on the local circuit, a footnote everywhere else. Let's dig in.

The Cinra saga began at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai with 2 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in GS U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Cinra's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Cinra plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Cinra is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. On the doubles front, Cinra has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in GD U17 — we hope Angelina Lin Wu has emotionally recovered.

Cinra has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Cinra has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Cinra showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in GS U17, which means Cinra traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in GD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Cinra entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 17th. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Cinra paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 10 entries, and 9 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Cinra looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Cinra's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Cinra has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Cinra. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. claire_hong.roast:

A player who has entered 26 events across 10 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 26-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Claire's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Claire keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Claire saga began at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Claire has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Claire's texts. The go-to partner is Hailey Yeseul Kim (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in XD U15 — we hope Adithya S Burisetty has emotionally recovered.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Claire is not just losing locally — Claire is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Hong family travels cross-country so Claire can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Claire has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Claire showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in GS U15, which means Claire traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Bellevue NW ORC, where Claire entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Claire paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Claire keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Claire has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Claire, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Claire. We will be here, taking notes.

claire_qiuchen_li.name: Claire QiuChen Li
claire_qiuchen_li.summary:
Hailing from Texas, Claire QiuChen Li is a U.S. Junior National Champion — crowned at the biggest event on the domestic calendar in GD U11. That title represents the culmination of grueling competition against the very best in the country. With 7 career victories across 20 tournaments, Claire has proven the ability to deliver when it matters most.

Notably, Claire has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Claire is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 6 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals champion (GD U11) • 5 ORC titles.

Claire has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 11 different players across the career:
  • Krishnavi Kasu (current primary partner) — 11 events together this season alone with 4 titles, building serious momentum (13 total events in GD/U13, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Kevin Xie (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U13/XD (9 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Eli You — 3 events in XD, best: #4, across 2 seasons

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 3 titles across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Claire is still ascending.

Claire has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Claire has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 15 seeded entries across the career, Claire consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 7 titles, Claire is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
claire_qiuchen_li.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Claire QiuChen Li, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. And yes, Claire IS a Junior National Champion — GD U11 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 58-entry career across 20 tournaments with a 12% win rate, which means 51 times — 51! — Claire went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 58 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Claire saga began at 2023 South ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2024 DFW South OLC in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Claire's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Claire experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Claire has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Claire does own 7 actual titles, so it is not like Claire has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 7 wins means Claire is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Claire plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 6 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Claire can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Claire has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Claire's texts. The most successful partnership is with Krishnavi Kasu — 13 events, 6 titles — and honestly, Krishnavi Kasu deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Krishnavi Kasu a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U13 — we hope Kevin Xie has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Claire entered 3 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-9. The Selection committee looked at Claire's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Claire and said 'nah.' Then there is the seeding situation. Claire has been seeded in 15 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Claire takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in GS U11, and finishing 17th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Claire. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Claire has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Claire is not just losing locally — Claire is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 12% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Li family travels cross-country so Claire can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Claire has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Claire showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in GS U13, which means Claire traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Claire entered 3 events with results ranging from 3rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 23rd, which is a polite way of saying Claire paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 7 titles to the name, 58 entries, and 51 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Claire looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Claire's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Claire has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Claire. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

clarinda_jin.name: Clarinda Jin
clarinda_jin.summary:

Clarinda is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Clarinda competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 3 singles titles, 5 doubles titles, 5 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

  • Brendan Chen (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in Mixed/U17/XD (6 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Noah Chien (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (3 total events in XD)

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 13 titles, Clarinda is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.

The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Clarinda entered 13 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Clarinda's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Clarinda and said 'nah.' Then there is the seeding situation. Clarinda has been seeded in 17 events and underperformed the seeding 5 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Clarinda takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC in GS U17, and finishing 17th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Clarinda. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Clarinda of is playing it safe. Clarinda has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Clarinda has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 12 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Clarinda has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Clarinda is not just losing locally — Clarinda is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 14% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Jin family travels cross-country so Clarinda can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Clarinda has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Clarinda showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U13, which means Clarinda traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 U.S. Selection Event in U15 GS. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Clarinda entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Clarinda paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 13 titles to the name, 92 entries, and 79 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Clarinda looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Clarinda's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Clarinda has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Clarinda. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

clay_yang.name: Clay Yang
clay_yang.summary:
Out of California, Clay Yang has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Clay's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Clay has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Clay especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (6 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (8 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

With 19 different doubles partners over the years, Clay adapts to anyone:
  • Yichen Li (current primary partner) — 12 events, 1 title together in BD/U13/U17, spanning 3 seasons
  • Anya Kuan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events)
  • Yifan Liu — a trusted BD partner (5 events, best: #2)

Clay has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Clay has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 6 seasons and 2 titles, Clay has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
clay_yang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Clay Yang, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 68 career entries in 29 tournaments, Clay has 2 titles — a 3% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 33 entries where Clay drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 68 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Clay saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2022 NW Oregon OLC in BS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Clay's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 19 entries — a stretch where Clay actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Clay is 0-for-11. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Clay plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Clay can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 19 of them, to be precise. Clay has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Clay's texts. The go-to partner is Yichen Li (12 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 SoCal ORC in BD U15 — we hope Sean Li has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Clay's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Clay entered 6 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Clay's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Clay and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Clay has shown up 8 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Clay has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Clay is not just losing locally — Clay is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yang family travels cross-country so Clay can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Clay has 39 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Clay showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U15, which means Clay traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Clay entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Clay paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 68 entries, and 66 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Clay looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Clay's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Clay has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Clay. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Clovee, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

connor_mills.name: Connor Mills
connor_mills.summary:
Representing Illinois, Connor Mills is a multi-time champion on the junior circuit, currently competing at the U19 level, capturing 2 titles across 15 tournaments and 38 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Connor keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Notably, Connor has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Connor especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #3, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (1 entry) • Junior Nationals competitor (4 entries).

With 12 different doubles partners over the years, Connor adapts to anyone:
  • Vedaant Sreedharan (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Zoey Tian — 2 events, 1 title together in XD
  • Veronica Ornatsky — a trusted XD partner (6 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)

Connor has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Connor isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 2 titles, Connor is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
connor_mills.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Connor Mills, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-33. Top-33 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 38 career entries in 15 tournaments, Connor has 2 titles — a 5% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 18 entries where Connor drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 38 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Connor saga began at 2023 Midwest OLC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 Midwest CRC in BD U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Connor's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 13 entries — a stretch where Connor actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Connor is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Connor would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Connor has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Connor has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Connor's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Connor plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Connor is 0-for-14. Zero titles in 14 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Connor and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Connor needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Connor has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Connor's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Veronica Ornatsky: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Veronica Ornatsky has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U17 — we hope Aaron Jin has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Connor's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Connor has shown up 4 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Connor has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Connor has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Connor has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Connor showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U17, which means Connor traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Connor entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 49th. That is an average placement of 41st, which is a polite way of saying Connor paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 38 entries, and 36 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Connor looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Connor's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Connor has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Connor. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Cory, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

crystal_sun.name: Crystal Sun
crystal_sun.summary:
A California competitor, Crystal Sun broke through with a tournament title, currently competing at the U13 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 51 entries across 19 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Crystal is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

With 11 different doubles partners over the years, Crystal adapts to anyone:
  • Aiden Wu (current primary partner) — 12 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (13 total events in XD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Anyashree Anand (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Hailey Lam — 2 events in GD, best: #3

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 1 title across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Crystal is still ascending.

Crystal has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Crystal has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Crystal is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
crystal_sun.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Crystal Sun, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Crystal has 51 career entries across 19 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 2% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Crystal saga began at 2023 SoCal ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2026 Dave Freeman Jr. SoCal OLC in XD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Crystal's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Crystal would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 5 times Crystal has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 5 times Crystal has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Crystal's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Crystal plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Crystal is 0-for-19. Zero titles in 19 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Crystal and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Crystal needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Crystal has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Crystal's texts. The go-to partner is Aiden Wu (13 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in XD U13 — we hope Aiden Wu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Crystal's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Crystal has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Crystal is not just losing locally — Crystal is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Sun family travels cross-country so Crystal can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-49 finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Crystal has 27 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Crystal showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13, which means Crystal traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in GS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Crystal entered 3 events with results ranging from 25th to 49th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Crystal paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 51 entries, and 50 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Crystal looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Crystal's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Crystal has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Crystal. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

2025 YONEX Dave Freeman Jr. SoCal OLC

So here we are: 4 seasons, 42 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Cynthia. Cynthia just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Cynthia has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Cynthia. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Cynthia: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

cyrus_cai.name: Cyrus Cai
cyrus_cai.summary:
Out of New York, Cyrus Cai has stepped onto the competitive stage with 2 tournament entries. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Cyrus's.

Cyrus competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
cyrus_cai.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Cyrus Cai, from New York, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Cyrus is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 Midwest ORC, and so far, the only thing Cyrus has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Cyrus saga began at 2023 Midwest ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Cyrus has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Cyrus showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U11, which means Cyrus traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2023 Midwest ORC in BD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Cyrus entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 17th. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Cyrus paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Cyrus is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Cyrus wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Cyrus, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

cyrus_m_cai.name: Cyrus M Cai
cyrus_m_cai.summary:
A New York competitor, Cyrus M Cai knows the feeling of standing atop the podium, currently competing at the U13 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 28 entries across 12 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Notably, Cyrus has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Cyrus especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries).

Cyrus has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 8 different players across the career:
  • Tailong Cheng (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (7 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)

Cyrus has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Cyrus isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Cyrus brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
cyrus_m_cai.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Cyrus M Cai, from New York, currently competing at the U13 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 28 career entries in 12 tournaments, Cyrus has 1 title — a 4% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 27 entries where Cyrus drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 28 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Cyrus saga began at 2023 NE ORC (U11) with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE NJBC CRC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Cyrus's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 14 entries — a stretch where Cyrus actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Cyrus is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Cyrus plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Cyrus is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Cyrus has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Cyrus's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Tailong Cheng: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Tailong Cheng has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BD U13 — we hope Tailong Cheng has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Cyrus's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Cyrus entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Cyrus's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Cyrus and said 'nah.'

Cyrus has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Cyrus has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Cyrus has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Cyrus showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U13, which means Cyrus traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Cyrus entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Cyrus paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 28 entries, and 27 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Cyrus looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Cyrus's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Cyrus has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Cyrus. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.
